coredata.dc:title: Decoration of carbon dots and AgCl over g-C3N4 nanosheets: Novel photocatalysts with substantially improved activity under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Abedi, Marjan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report new ternary photocatalysts by decoration of carbon dots and AgCl over g-C3N4 nanosheets (gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl) through simple procedure and they were characterized by a couple of techniques including X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface analysis, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Compared with the corresponding pure and binary photocatalysts, the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites exhibited efficient degradation activities toward RhB, MB, MO, and phenol under visible light and demonstrated no significant loss of photocatalytic performance after five runs. The gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposite with loading 20% of AgCl displayed the highest activity, which is as much as 28.5, 33.6, 40.4, and 10.7 times of the pure g-C3N4 in photodegradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol, respectively. Finally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for the substantially improved photocatalytic performances was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4-NS/CDs/AgCl
Carbon dots
Visible-light response
Pollutant degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl.
(a) EDX spectra for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) samples. (b-g) EDX mapping of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite for different elements.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, and (c) HRTEM images of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite: (a) survey scan, and high-resolution spectra for: (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Ag 3d, and (f) Cl 2p.

(a) FT-IR spectra for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposites. (b) UV–vis DRS for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), AgCl, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl. (c) Plots of (αhv)2 versus hv for the samples.
TG analysis of the AgCl, gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), CDs, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) samples.

(a) Photodegradation of RhB over the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), AgCl, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl. (b) The degradation rate constants of RhB over gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), AgCl, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl.
PL spectra for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (30%) nanocomposites.
A plausible diagram for separation of e−/h+ pairs in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite in presence of various scavengers. (b) Reusability of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite. (c) The degradation rate constants of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol over the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) samples under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration for preparation of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites.
The amounts of gCN-NS/CDs, AgNO3, and NaCl applied for preparation of the ternary nanocomposites.
Weight percentages of different elements in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite.
The textural properties of gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Decoration of carbon dots and AgCl over g-C3N4 nanosheets: Novel photocatalysts with substantially improved activity under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report new ternary photocatalysts by decoration of carbon dots and AgCl over g-C3N4 nanosheets (gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl) through simple procedure and they were characterized by a couple of techniques including X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface analysis, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Compared with the corresponding pure and binary photocatalysts, the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites exhibited efficient degradation activities toward RhB, MB, MO, and phenol under visible light and demonstrated no significant loss of photocatalytic performance after five runs. The gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposite with loading 20% of AgCl displayed the highest activity, which is as much as 28.5, 33.6, 40.4, and 10.7 times of the pure g-C3N4 in photodegradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol, respectively. Finally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for the substantially improved photocatalytic performances was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the huge development of industry and civilization worldwide, water contamination and scarcely have gradually become more serious challenges [1–4]. Nowadays, the increasing awareness about environmental crisis has motivated extensive efforts to develop cost-effective technologies to manipulate this drawback [3–5]. It has been confirmed that semiconductor-based photocatalysis is an effective approach for environmental remediation [3–8]. As a consequence, photocatalytic processes have attracted great interests from the research communities and couple of photocatalysts have been introduced, which they are activated by UV and visible light irradiations [5–13]. With the aim of effective utilization of the solar irradiation in the photocatalytic processes, fabrication of visible-light-driven (VLD) photocatalysts have gained much more interests [9–17].

In 2009, Wang et al. introduced graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, abbreviated as gCN) as a cheap and environmentally-friendly semiconductor with high photocatalytic performance under visible light [18]. This polymeric semiconductor has considerable thermal and chemical stabilities [19,20]. Nevertheless, utilization of bulk gCN in the photocatalytic processes has greatly limited, owing to its small surface area, severe recombination of the photoinduced electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs, and poor photo responsivity in visible region [20–22]. To address these drawbacks, several strategies including elemental (metallic and nonmetallic) doping, integration with small/large band gap semiconductors, and fabrication nanostructures of gCN have been proposed [21–37].

It has been proved that exfoliated gCN has high specific surface and improved photocatalytic performance [38]. Thus, in the present work, nanosheets of gCN (abbreviated as gCN-NS) were prepared by a simple heating method [39,40]. In recent years, different promising photocatalysts have been derived from silver halides [41–43]. Among the silver halides, silver chloride (AgCl) is a wide band gap semiconductor with an indirect band gap of 3.25 eV, displaying some appealing properties [44–46]. Considering the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) energy levels of AgCl as +3.19 and −0.06 eV, respectively, this semiconductor was combined with the fabricated gCN-NS to help separation of the produced charges in the gCN-NS photocatalyst under visible light [41]. To further retardation of the charge carriers from recombination and with the purpose of obtaining high-performance gCN-based photocatalyst, carbon dots (CDs) were used as electron mediator between gCN-NS and AgCl semiconductors [47]. In other words, novel ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites were fabricated with the aim of providing substantially improved photocatalytic activity under visible light. Although fabrication and investigation about photocatalytic activity of VLD photocatalysts based on gCN have been recently attracted some interests from the research community, in the best of our knowledge, there is no report about preparation of ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites. The fabricated photocatalysts were extensively characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDX), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface analysis, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The synthesized ternary nanocomposites displayed excellent photocatalytic performance in degradations of four pollutants including two cationic dyes (RhB and MB), one anionic dye (MO) and one colorless pollutant (phenol). Interestingly, the ternary nanocomposite displayed excellent activity in degradations of these contaminants when compared with the corresponding binary photocatalysts and pristine gCN. Effects of AgCl loading, calcination temperature, and scavengers on the photocatalytic performance were explored. Moreover, a mechanism for the improved photocatalytic performance was proposed.

The XRD analysis was carried out with Cu Kα radiation using a Philips Xpert X-ray diffractometer. A SEM instrument (LEO 1430VP) using an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and a TEM and HRTEM instrument (JEOL, JEM-2100F) with accelerating voltage of 200 kV was employed for the morphology observations. The EDX spectra were provided by the same SEM. The XPS was performed on a Thermo Scientifi (ESCALAB 250Xi) system with Mg X-ray source. The TGA was conducted on Linseis STA PT 1000 apparatus at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 (25–700 °C in air). The DRS spectra of samples were measured by Scinco 4100 apparatus. The FT-IR spectra were acquired by a Perkin Elmer Spectrum RX I instrument. The PL spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer (LS 55) fluorescence spectrophotometer. Textural properties of the photocatalysts were examined by a Micrometrics Tristar II Plus adsorption apparatus at −196 °C. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was conducted on a Cecile 9000 spectrophotometer. For the ultrasonic irradiations, a Bandelin ultrasonic instrument (9HD 3100) was used (probe diameter = 12 mm, Ti horn, 75 W, working frequency = 20 kHz).

The bulk gCN was synthesized by heating melamine (Loba Chemie, 99.2%), according to the reported procedure [48]. Briefly, 6 g melamine was heated to 500 °C for 2 h and then heated to 520 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. For preparation of the gCN-NS photocatalyst, 2 g of the bulk gCN was powdered in a mortar and it was spread in three petri dishes with a diameter of almost 10 cm. Thereafter, the powder was calcined in air at 520 °C for 2 h [49,50].

For preparation of CDs, 3 g citric acid (Merck, 99%) and 3 g urea (Merck, 99%) were added to 10 mL water to form a transparent solution. The solution was irradiated for 5 min in a domestic 750 W microwave oven. In this process, the solution changed from a colorless liquid to a dark-brown solid. The resultant solid was heated at 60 °C for 1 h in an oven to remove the volatile molecules. Afterwards, the resultant powder of CDs was dissolved in 180 mL of water. Then, the suspension was centrifuged (7500 rmin−1, 10 min) to remove agglomerated particles of the CDs solution [51]. The resulting brown solution was applied for preparation of the gCN/CDs nanocomposite. Briefly, 0.4 g of the bulk gCN and 0.5 mL of CDs were dispersed in 100 mL of water with ultrasonic irradiation for 5 min. Then, the resultant suspension dried in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite was prepared by thermal exfoliation of the prepared gCN/CDs nanocomposite. In a typical procedure, 1 g of the gCN/CDs nanocomposite was placed in an open alumina crucible and heated by rate of 10 °C min−1 and stopped at 520 °C for 2 h [52]. The color of the resultant photocatalyst was light yellow.

For preparation 0.5 g of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, where 20% is weight percentage of AgCl, 0.4 g of the gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite was dispersed in 150 mL water by ultrasonication for 5 min. Then, 0.118 g AgNO3 (Loba Chemie, 99.2%) was added to the suspension and magnetically stirred for 2 h to enable enough adsorption of Ag+ ions on the surface of gCN nanosheets. The surface charge of gCN is negative around pH 7 [53,54], leading to considerable adsorption of Ag+ ions over the negatively charges gCN. Afterwards, 20 mL of NaCl solution (0.041 g in 20 mL of water) was dropped into the suspension under stirring for 1 h. The final yellow precipitate was gathered by centrifugation, washed two times with water and ethanol, and then dried in oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The resultant ternary photocatalysts were denoted as gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (5%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (10%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (30%) for the AgCl feeding amounts of 5%, 10%, 20%, and 30%, respectively. The amounts of gCN-NS/CDs, AgNO3, and NaCl applied for preparation of the ternary nanocomposites with different weight percentages of AgCl are listed in Table 1 . For comparison, the gCN-NS/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite was synthesized in the same way in the absence of CDs. For this purpose, 0.4 g of the gCN-NS nanocomposite was dispersed in 150 mL of water by ultrasonication for 5 min. Then, 0.118 g AgNO3 was added to the suspension and magnetically stirred for 2 h. Afterwards, 20 mL of NaCl solution (0.041 g in 20 mL of water) was dropped into the suspension under stirring for 1 h. The final light yellow precipitate was gathered by centrifugation, washed two times with water and ethanol, and then dried in oven at 60 °C for 24 h. The preparation procedure of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Photocatalytic activity of the fabricated materials was measured by degradations of the pollutants in a cylindrical glassy reactor with water circulation system to maintain the temperature at 25 °C. The reactor was irradiated with a 50 W LED lamp and the distance between the light source and the reactor was about 20 cm. The LED source has intensive emission in the visible region without any emissions in UV and IR ranges [55]. In each run, 0.1 g of the photocatalyst was dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 6 min. Then, 250 mL of the pollutants and the photocatalyst were poured into the reactor, in which air was bubbling continuously from the bottom of the reactor and reactor mounted on a mechanical stirrer to keep the suspension homogeneous. The suspension was kept in dark for 60 min to reach adsorption/desorption process and then the lamp was switched on to initiate the reaction. During the study, samples were withdrawn, centrifuged subsequently, and the absorptions were measured at the maximum absorption wavelengths of 553, 664, 477, and 270 nm for RhB (2.5 × 10−5 M), MB (2.5 × 10−5 M), MO (2.5 × 10−5 M), and phenol (5 × 10−5 M), respectively.

To investigate the role of reactive species in the photocatalytic process, photodegradation of RhB over the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite was tested under visible-light, employing 1 mM of 2-propanol (2-PrOH), p-benzoquinone (BQ), and ammonium oxalate (AO) as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals (
OH), superoxide anion radical (
O2 −), and hole (h+), respectively [56].

The XRD analysis was applied to detect the crystalline structure of gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl samples with different compositions and they are displayed in Fig. 1 . For the bulk gCN and gCN-NS samples, the in-planar weak peak at 13.5° corresponds to the (1 0 0) plane and the interlayer-stacking sharp peak at 27.5° corresponds to the (0 0 2) plane (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [57]. For the gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite, the same pattern was observed, demonstrating that the structure of gCN-NS has not changed by introducing CDs and no diffraction peaks of CDs were observed, due to its limited quantity in the nanocomposite [58]. In the XRD patterns of gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites, besides the characteristic peaks of gCN-NS, the peaks for AgCl were recognized at 2θ = 27.82° (1 1 1), 32.24° (2 0 0), 46.23° (2 2 0), 54.82° (3 1 1), 57.47° (2 2 2), 67.47° (4 0 0), 74.47° (3 3 1), and 76.73° (4 2 0) planes (JCPDS No. 31-1238) [59], implying that the ternary nanocomposites have been successfully formed. No diffraction peaks corresponding to the possible impurities were detected in the patterns, demonstrating that the nanocomposites have high purity.

The elemental compositions of the photocatalysts were examined by EDX analysis, as the spectra are presented in Fig. 2 a. The spectra for the gCN and gCN-NS photocatalysts show the presence of C and N elements, whereas C, N, and O elements were observed in the spectrum of gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite. Besides, C, N, Ag, and Cl elements were clearly observed in the gCN-NS/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite and C, N, O, Ag, and Cl elements in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite. Hence, in accordance with the XRD analysis, the EDX spectra indicated that the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposite have been successfully fabricated through the proposed simple route. As clearly observed in the EDX spectra of the photocatalysts containing CDs, the large amounts of oxygen element are seen in these spectra, because of the presence of different functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy in the CDs [60]. Also to observe the elemental distributions in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, EDX mapping was performed (Fig. 2(b-g)). Also, the weight percentages of different elements have been gathered in Table 2 .

To observe morphology of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, its SEM and TEM images were provided, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a and b). In the SEM image (Fig. 3a), the deposited particles over graphitic carbon nitride are observed. In Fig. 3b, the TEM image shows that over very thin layer of gCN-NS, particles of AgCl with size of nearly 25 nm and particles of CDs with size of about 5 nm have deposited. As confirmed by HRTEM image of the ternary nanocomposite (Fig. 3c), particles of CDs and AgCl have loaded onto surfaces of the gCN-NS. Here again, the HRTEM micrograph of the ternary nanocomposite presented the interplanar spacing of 0.325 nm [35], 0.320 nm [58], and 0.277 nm [61] corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of gCN-NS, (0 0 2) plane of CDs, and (2 0 0) plane of AgCl, respectively (Fig. 3c).

To determine chemical status of elements in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, XPS analysis was considered. Fig. 4 presents the XPS results, including full spectrum and the spectra for C, O, N, Ag, and Cl elements of this ternary nanocomposite. As observed in the survey scan, no characteristic peaks for any elements other than C, O, N, Ag, and Cl were discovered, confirming purity of the ternary photocatalyst (Fig. 4a). In the case of C 1s spectrum, the peaks at binding energies of 284.85 and 288.16 eV are belonged to sp2 C C bonds and sp2 bonded carbon in N-containing aromatic rings (NCN) of gCN-NS, respectively [62]. In addition, the peaks centered at 286.4 and 293.63 eV correspond to COH/COC and CO groups, respectively, indicating the presence of CDs particles (Fig. 4b) [63–65]. In addition, the peak at 286.4 eV is related to the adventitious carbon contamination species from XPS measurement. The spectrum of O 1s shows three components at binding energies of 531.8, 533.02, and 534.1 eV, which are attributed to the chemical environments of COH/COC, CO, and HOH groups, respectively (Fig. 4c) [66]. The N 1s XPS signal can be fitted by a mixture of three peaks located at 398.64, 400.26, and 404.67 eV, which exhibit nitrogen atoms in the CNC, N(C)3, and CNH functional groups, existing in the structure of gCN-NS (Fig. 4d) [67]. The Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 peaks were observed at binding energies of 367.96 and 373.96 eV, belonging to Ag+ of AgCl [68]. In Fig. 4f, two peaks present at binging energies of 198.24 and 199.87 eV, which are ascribed to the characteristic peaks of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 in AgCl counterpart [69].

The characteristic vibrations for functional groups of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) samples were identified by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5 a). In these spectra, the broad band in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1 is ascribed to surface-adsorbed hydroxyl and NH groups. Several peaks in the range of 1240–1640 cm−1 are related to the skeletal vibrations of aromatic CN heterocycles, while the absorption peak located at 808 cm−1 is belonged to the breathing mode of s-triazine units [70]. In the edge of CDs, functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and unoxidized hydrocarbons are existed [52,72]. Hence, for the nanocomposite with CDs, the peak at 1372 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibration of CH and the peak at 1622 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO group [51,71]. Compared with the pure gCN, the nanocomposites have similar absorption peaks, confirming that the exfoliation process and the introduction of CDs and AgCl particles did not destroy the gCN structure. Similar to AgCl-based photocatalysts, there is not any absorption peak for Ag-Cl bond [72].

UV–vis DRS spectra can provide significant information about the ability of photocatalysts to generate e−/h+ pairs under the light illumination. The UV–vis DRS spectra of gCN, gCN-NS, AgCl, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl samples were recorded, as presented in Fig. 5b. The pure gCN shows an absorption edge of 490 nm, signifying its photocatalytic performance under visible light. Compared to the gCN, the gCN-NS exhibits a slight blue-shift, confirming ultrathin characteristic of the exfoliated gCN-NS [58,73]. The blue shift in the electronic spectra of nanomaterials is a usual behavior due to quantum confinement effect [74]. In comparison with the gCN, it is observed that the gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite has slightly improved absorption in the visible region, confirming integration of CDs with the gCN-NS [47]. As clearly observed, there is not much difference in the light absorption ability of the gCN-NS/CDs and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites in the visible region, due to large band gap of AgCl [75]. According to the results of UV–vis DRS, the fabricated gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites displayed slightly enhanced absorption in visible region, which is useful for improving photocatalytic performance under visible light.

It is well known that the band gap energy (Eg) of the resultant photocatalysts can be estimated using the following equation:
(1)
α h υ = β
( h υ -
E
g
)
n / 2
in which α, h, υ, β, and Eg are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively. In this equation, the value of n depends on the characteristics of the transition. The values of n are 1 and 4 for direct and indirect transitions, respectively (the value of n for AgCl and gCN are 4 and 1, respectively) [76]. In this method, the Eg values were calculated by extrapolation of the linear parts of the curves obtained by plotting (αhυ)2 versus hv, as shown in Fig. 5c. It is obvious that band gap of the prepared photocatalysts are between 2.7 (for gCN) and 3.2 eV (for AgCl). In the case of gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, there is an increase in its band gap, due to formation of a heterojunction between gCN with narrow band gap and AgCl with wide band gap.

In this part, TG analysis was performed to evaluate thermal stability of the samples from room temperature to 700 °C and the corresponding curves are illustrated in Fig. 6 . For the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), gCN-NS/CDs, and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites with different compositions, a two-step decrease of weights is observed. In the initial step, small weight loss is observed up to 140 °C, due to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules over the photocatalysts and in the second step, considerable weight loss is observed mainly in the range of 400–680 °C, due to combustion of graphitic carbon nitride parts of the samples. As confirmed in the literature, graphitic carbon nitride is an organic semiconductor and it starts to decompose by heating upper that nearly 490 °C [53]. As observed, the gCN powder is stable below 490 °C under airflow and its decomposition started after this temperature and completed at ∼650 °C. However, the thermostability of gCN was decreased after converting to gCN-NS and integration with CDs and AgCl particles. For the gCN-NS, binary, and ternary photocatalysts, the enhanced surface area through exfoliation and loaded particles could act as active sites for adsorption of oxygen from the surrounding, leading to oxidizing gCN part of the photocatalysts at lower temperatures than the pristine gCN [77]. Furthermore, by heating AgCl up to 700 °C, there was almost no change in its weight. This thermal stability is due to the lack of organic part in the AgCl sample. For comparison purpose, TG analysis was also done for CDs suspension. As can be seen, a fast weight loss is observed up to 90 °C. This rapid decrease of the weight is related to evaporation of water. In addition, the slow weight loss is attributed to burning of CDs at higher temperatures. Finally, organic parts of the photocatalysts were calculated from the weight remainders after heating them up to 700 °C. The organic contents in the gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (5%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (10%), gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (30%) nanocomposites were determined to be 76, 92.3, 88.1, 78.4 and 67.9 wt%, respectively.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) photocatalysts were used to measure their textural properties, as the results are displayed in Fig. 7 and Table 3 . As observed, specific surface area of the gCN-NS (88.08 m2 g−1) is 6 times higher than that of the gCN (14.56 m2 g−1), indicating high degree of exfoliation. However, after depositing CDs and AgCl particles over the surface of gCN-NS, its specific surface area considerably decreased. This decrease could be attributed to covering of the gCN-NS surface by deposited particles, which may block some of the active sites of the gCN-NS sample by CDs and AgCl counterparts [52].

The visible-light photocatalytic performances of the fabricated samples were explored by the degradation of RhB and the results are displayed in Fig. 8 a. As clearly shown, a blank test (in the absence of the photocatalysts) shows that the photolysis of RhB is negligible. The photoactivity of gCN is very low, with only 36% of degradation after 120 min. When the gCN-NS and gCN/CDs samples were employed as photocatalysts, the degradation efficiencies of RhB were increased to 44.7 and 76.7% within 120 min, respectively. In contrast, with loading of CDs and AgCl particles over the gCN-NS, remarkable enhancement of RhB degradation is observed and the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite displayed superior activity by complete degradation of RhB after the light irradiation for 120 min. Hence, it was concluded that integration of gCN-NS with CDs and AgCl has substantial effect on the photocatalytic performance. For comparison, photocatalytic activity of the pure AgCl was investigated. Due to its large band gap and consequently poor absorption of visible light, its photocatalytic activity was very low. Fig. 8b illustrates the degradation rate constants of RhB over the fabricated samples. As observed, the degradation rate constant of RhB over the gCN-NS (60.1 × 10−4 min−1) was substantially improved after loading CDs and AgCl and the ternary nanocomposite with 20 wt% of AgCl showed the maximal degradation rate constant of 308.73 × 10−4 min−1, which is 28.5, 5.1, 2.3, and 3.3 times greater than the gCN (10.84 × 10−4 min−1), gCN-NS (60.1 × 10−4 min−1), gCN-NS/CDs (137 × 10−4 min−1), and gCN-NS/AgCl (20%) (137 × 10−4 min−1) samples.

The UV–vis spectra of RhB during the degradation reactions on the gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) photocatalysts were provided in the range of 400–700 nm and the spectra are presented in Fig. S1(a–d). As figures depicted, the characteristic absorption of this pollutant continually decreases with the light exposure and complete degradation takes place only over the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite.

To illustrate the charge carrier transfer characteristics of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/AgCl (20%), and gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) photocatalysts, their PL spectra were provided (Fig. 9 ). Since intensity of peaks in PL spectra originates from recombination of the charge carriers, a lower PL intensity indicates lower recombination of e−/h+ pairs. As presented in Fig. 9, the emission intensity of all samples was reduced as compared with the bulk gCN, and the ternary nanocomposite with 20 wt% of AgCl displayed the minimum emission intensity, indicating maximum reduction in recombination of the e−/h+ pairs. This suggests that in the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, there is less opportunity for the charge carriers to recombine, facilitating the migration of them to reach surface of the photocatalyst and participate in the degradation reactions. As a results, the PL spectra confirm that integration of gCN-NS with CDs and AgCl plays an essential role in suppression of the charge carriers from recombination, which is benefit for the photocatalytic performance.

To further understand the improved photocatalytic performance of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites, a mechanism was proposed and it was schematically presented in Fig. 10 . In order to clarify separation of the charge carriers over the ternary nanocomposites, it is necessary to find out the CB and VB potentials of the components. These energy levels were calculated using the following equations:
(2)
E
CB
= χ -
E
e
- 0.5
E
g
(3)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E
g
where EVB and ECB are the VB and CB potentials, respectively. Moreover, Ee is the energy of free electrons vs. hydrogen (4.5 eV) [78]. Finally, χ is the electronegativity of semiconductor and it was calculated by the following equation:
(4)
χ =
[ χ
( A )
a
χ
( B )
b
]
1 / a + b
In which a, and b, and c are the number of atoms in the compounds [31]. The values of Eg and χ for gCN are 2.70 and 4.73 eV, respectively. Therefore, the ECB and EVB of gCN were calculated to be −1.13 and +1.57 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). In addition, Eg and χ values for AgCl are 3.25 and 6.06 eV, respectively. Furthermore, ECB and EVB for AgCl were calculated to be −0.06 and +3.19 eV/NHE, respectively. In photocatalytic processes, the initial step is absorption of photons with an energy higher or equal to the band gap. When the suspension containing certain amount of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites is exposed to visible light, a large number of e−/h+ pairs are generated over gCN semiconductor, because of its low band gap. As observed, the CB of gCN is more negative than the CB of AgCl, so the photoinduced electrons in the gCN counterpart of the nanocomposite rapidly transfer to the CB of AgCl through CDs as mediators, while the photogenerated holes remain on the VB of gCN. Hence, the charge carriers are spatially separated from each other, leading to the suppression of them from recombination, as confirmed by PL spectra and photocatalytic activities. The collected electrons over the CB of AgCl subsequently react with adsorbed molecules of oxygen to produce OH
radicals (E0(O2/H2O2) = +0.682 eV/NHE) [79]. Additionally, some of the electrons on the CB of gCN react with adsorbed molecules of O2 to produce
O2 − (E0(O2/
O2 −) = −0.33 eV/NHE) [80]. On the other hand, the photoinduced holes on the VB of gCN degrade RhB molecules. Based on the characterization results and photocatalytic experiments, the substantially improved photocatalytic performance of the ternary nanocomposites was attributed to the more production of e−/h+ pairs and efficiently separation of the photoinduced charges from recombination. As observed from BET characterization, specific surface area of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite is lower than that of the gCN-NS sample. Although specific surface area of photocatalysts generally have considerable influence in the photocatalytic processes, in the case of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite, it was observed that the improved photocatalytic performance is mainly related to more harvesting of visible light and remarkably suppression of the charge carriers from recombination, rather than the surface area.

In this study, AO, 2-PrOH, and BQ were used as the scavengers for h+,
OH, and
O2 −, respectively, to elucidate the role of these active species in the degradation reaction. As shown in Fig. 11 a, when BQ was introduced in the reaction system, the degradation rate constant was reduced from 309 × 10−4 min−1 to 11.2 × 10−4 min−1, confirming that superoxide anion radicals are the main activated species. But, hydroxyl radicals and holes have low effect on the degradation reaction of RhB, because the degradation rate constants change slightly after the addition of them in the reaction system. Additionally, degradation rate constant of RhB was considerably decreased by N2 bubbling in the reaction system, confirming the important role of dissolved O2 in scavenging electrons.

For successful utilization of a photocatalyst, it should have considerable stability during the degradation cycles without significant loss of activity. As indicated in Fig. 11b, the reusability of gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite was evaluated during five successive experiments. As observed, the photocatalyst did not exhibit significant deactivation in these consecutive cycles, indicating that the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite has high stability in the photocatalytic degradation reaction.

Complete removal of different organic dyes from wastewaters is a challenging task, because of much differences in their stabilities. As a consequence, the photocatalytic degradations of four pollutants including two cationic dyes (RhB and MB), one anionic dye (MO), and one colorless pollutant (phenol) under visible light were applied to evaluate the photocatalytic performances of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite in comparison with its counterparts (Fig. 11c). It can be seen that photocatalytic performance of the gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite is almost 28.5, 33.6, 40.4, and 10.7 times premier of the gCN sample in degradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol pollutants, respectively. Furthermore, the improved activity of the ternary nanocomposite is 4.9, 14.4, 19.6, and 7.5-folds higher than the gCN-NS sample in degradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol, respectively. Up to now, different semiconductors such as Ag2SO4 [25], Ag2SO3 [28], Sm2O3 [34], Ag2WO4 [35], Ag3PO4 [48], Ag3VO4 [70], and SmVO4 [77] have been integrated with gCN to improve its photocatalytic performance. However, the ternary photocatalyst fabricated in this work has substantial activity in comparison with these photocatalysts. In addition, the amounts of high cost elements applied in the preparation of the present ternary photocatalysts are not high. Therefore, it was concluded that the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl (20%) nanocomposite is a fascinating VLD photocatalyst in degradations of different organic pollutants.

In summary, novel ternary gCN-NS/CDs/AgCl nanocomposites were synthesized by facile decoration of CDs and AgCl particles over the nanosheets of gCN. The substantially improved photocatalytic activity of the ternary nanocomposites in degradations of four pollutants including two cationic dyes (RhB and MB), one anionic dye (MO) and one colorless pollutant (phenol) under visible light was confirmed. The 20 wt% AgCl modified gCN-NS/CDs illustrated the highest photocatalytic efficiency, which was almost 28.5, 33.6, 40.4, and 10.7 times premier of the gCN sample in degradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol, respectively. Furthermore, the enhanced activity of the ternary nanocomposite was 4.9, 14.4, 19.6, and 7.5-folds higher than the gCN-NS sample in degradations of RhB, MB, MO, and phenol, respectively. The synergistic effects of AgCl, CDs, and gCN-NS counterparts in effectively separation of the charge carriers is the major parameter affecting on the photocatalytic performance. In addition, the ternary photocatalyst exhibited considerable stability in the degradation reaction, which could be a promising candidate for environmental remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, for financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2018.01.023.
